texts.label: text
texts.orig: The applications Elkem identified as potentially interesting for a gas permeation process are:
texts.text: The applications Elkem identified as potentially interesting for a gas permeation process are:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Isobutane separation from chloromethane to recycle the latter;
texts.text: Isobutane separation from chloromethane to recycle the latter;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Ammonia separation from hexamethyldisiloxane (HMDS) in nitrogen vector.
texts.text: Ammonia separation from hexamethyldisiloxane (HMDS) in nitrogen vector.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The interest behind this application is that currently Elkem spends 1 M per year for the gas treatment and the raw material needed to compensate the loss of HCl. The composition of the feed to treat is reported in Table 10:

texts.text: Table 10. Feed composition for the isobutane/chloromethane separation application

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the isobutane/chloromethane application the ratio of molar volumes is ~1.91 and the ratio of total solubility parameters ~1.4. The separation by permeation is therefore possible. Glassy polymers should preferentially allow the passage of chloromethane while isobutane will be kept in the retentate phase because of its molar volume. The opposite is true for rubbery polymers, which should allow preferentially the passage of the isobutane while keeping the chloromethane on the retentate side.
texts.text: For the isobutane/chloromethane application the ratio of molar volumes is ~1.91 and the ratio of total solubility parameters ~1.4. The separation by permeation is therefore possible. Glassy polymers should preferentially allow the passage of chloromethane while isobutane will be kept in the retentate phase because of its molar volume. The opposite is true for rubbery polymers, which should allow preferentially the passage of the isobutane while keeping the chloromethane on the retentate side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The advantage of a membrane that allows MeCl to permeate is that, in principle, it will be selective towards the other hydrocarbons.
texts.text: The advantage of a membrane that allows MeCl to permeate is that, in principle, it will be selective towards the other hydrocarbons.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rhône-Poulenc performed some experiments on the separation of the 2 molecules using a PVC membrane at ambient temperature and 4.5 bar of pressure difference (45). The feed composition was: 94% chloromethane, 6% isobutane (probably in volume). The results showed:
texts.text: Rhône-Poulenc performed some experiments on the separation of the 2 molecules using a PVC membrane at ambient temperature and 4.5 bar of pressure difference (45). The feed composition was: 94% chloromethane, 6% isobutane (probably in volume). The results showed:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2% isobutane in the permeate (probably in volume);

texts.orig: Based on these preliminary information, mass balances lead to a yield-selectivity graph against the percentage of feed flow permeated (Figure 16, case of a module with cross-flow) considering the inlet composition of Table 10.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 16. Chloromethane yield-selectivity plot versus the percentage of feed flow permeated in the case of a module with cross-flow
texts.text: Figure 16. Chloromethane yield-selectivity plot versus the percentage of feed flow permeated in the case of a module with cross-flow

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to the specification on the chloromethane purity needed, a percentage of feed flow permeated can be fixed and consequently the recovery yield is obtained. Knowing then the inlet flow rate and the membrane permeability  under  the  process  conditions,  the  size  of  the  membrane  can  be  calculated.  These  are  just preliminary calculations that are useful to visualise the level of performance that one can expect. More precise information is needed in order to develop the process correctly.
texts.text: According to the specification on the chloromethane purity needed, a percentage of feed flow permeated can be fixed and consequently the recovery yield is obtained. Knowing then the inlet flow rate and the membrane permeability  under  the  process  conditions,  the  size  of  the  membrane  can  be  calculated.  These  are  just preliminary calculations that are useful to visualise the level of performance that one can expect. More precise information is needed in order to develop the process correctly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the development of the separation solution Processium recommends the following steps:
texts.text: For the development of the separation solution Processium recommends the following steps:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Choose,  with  the  help  of  an  expert  (i.e.  LRGP/MEMSIC),  the  most  interesting  membranes  for  the separation of the two molecules;
texts.text: Choose,  with  the  help  of  an  expert  (i.e.  LRGP/MEMSIC),  the  most  interesting  membranes  for  the separation of the two molecules;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Perform permeability measurement to assess the permeate flow rate and the selectivity of the selected membranes (i.e. at LRGP/MEMSIC);
texts.text: Perform permeability measurement to assess the permeate flow rate and the selectivity of the selected membranes (i.e. at LRGP/MEMSIC);

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Simulate, i.e. using MEMSIC software, the gas permeation processes to find the optimal configuration in terms of number of stages and recycling;
texts.text: Simulate, i.e. using MEMSIC software, the gas permeation processes to find the optimal configuration in terms of number of stages and recycling;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Estimate CAPEX and OPEX of the process. It can be done with the help of the LRGP/MEMSIC or by Processium contacting the providers of the chosen membrane.
texts.text: Estimate CAPEX and OPEX of the process. It can be done with the help of the LRGP/MEMSIC or by Processium contacting the providers of the chosen membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For this second application the ratio of molar volumes is ~8.3 and the ratio of total solubility parameters ~2. Separation  by  permeation  is  therefore  possible.  Glassy  polymers  should  preferentially  allow  the  passage  of ammonia while HMDS will be kept in the retentate phase because of its molar volume. The opposite is true for rubbery polymers, which should allow preferentially the passage of HMDS while keeping the ammonia on the retentate side.
texts.text: For this second application the ratio of molar volumes is ~8.3 and the ratio of total solubility parameters ~2. Separation  by  permeation  is  therefore  possible.  Glassy  polymers  should  preferentially  allow  the  passage  of ammonia while HMDS will be kept in the retentate phase because of its molar volume. The opposite is true for rubbery polymers, which should allow preferentially the passage of HMDS while keeping the ammonia on the retentate side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For  the  development of the  separation solution the  same  steps  proposed for the chloromethane/isobutane mixture should be followed.
texts.text: For  the  development of the  separation solution the  same  steps  proposed for the chloromethane/isobutane mixture should be followed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas permeation, the latest method of separating gaseous mixtures, is now a mature technological solution to be considered for industrial applications. While the portfolio of materials and modules available, and the types of applications currently being developed, are still relatively limited and confined to membranes based on dense polymers, recent developments in nanostructured materials and thin-film manufacturing techniques (including additive manufacturing) point to breakthrough developments in the years to come (3).
texts.text: Gas permeation, the latest method of separating gaseous mixtures, is now a mature technological solution to be considered for industrial applications. While the portfolio of materials and modules available, and the types of applications currently being developed, are still relatively limited and confined to membranes based on dense polymers, recent developments in nanostructured materials and thin-film manufacturing techniques (including additive manufacturing) point to breakthrough developments in the years to come (3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While polymer materials have proved their worth in terms of performance (and will probably continue to be widely used), the arrival on the market of materials with very high selectivity, high permeance, and high chemical and thermal resistance (zeolites, silica, carbon molecular sieves, graphene, MOF) will certainly open up new areas and lead to new applications, particularly in the context of sustainable production and the circular economy (coupling  reactions/separations  at  high  temperature,  recovery  and  recycling  of  compounds,  treatment  of mixtures containing thermolabile molecules, etc.) (3).
texts.text: While polymer materials have proved their worth in terms of performance (and will probably continue to be widely used), the arrival on the market of materials with very high selectivity, high permeance, and high chemical and thermal resistance (zeolites, silica, carbon molecular sieves, graphene, MOF) will certainly open up new areas and lead to new applications, particularly in the context of sustainable production and the circular economy (coupling  reactions/separations  at  high  temperature,  recovery  and  recycling  of  compounds,  treatment  of mixtures containing thermolabile molecules, etc.) (3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  this  document  Processium  has,  in  a  preliminary  way,  analysed  the  applications  identified  by  Elkem  as interesting for a gas permeation separation process. Based on these preliminary observations, the separation appears  feasible.  However,  a  more  detailed  study  including  experimental  tests  should  be  carried  out  to determine the most suitable membranes, the expected performances and define the mass and energy balances to  determine  the  real  potential  of  the  gas  permeation  process  for  the  Elkem  applications.  In  this  sense, Processium identified and contacted a potential partner for this type of support (LRGP Nancy/MEMSIC).
